project_objective: Build an independent Rust-owned autonomous development runtime with a thin Code-OSS VSIX, supporting agent loop execution, external Mode Packs, runtime-enforced permissions, controlled tools, persistent state, and headless long-running development workflows.

strategic_capability_mapping.relationship: Removes diagnostics wrapper growth as a blocker before returning to M1 Agent Loop Integration.
Keeps future diagnostics API work tied to permission and safety semantics instead of wrapper-only reports.
Preserves the no-write/no-apply boundary while enforcing a clearer API surface for controlled tools.
Prevents unattended phase execution from spending more phases on reconstructable diagnostics wrappers.

questions.question: Which strategic_capabilities does this phase advance?
What new user-visible or runtime capability does it add?
Why can existing functionality not substitute for this work?
What concrete product gap remains if this is not implemented?
How does this move Brownie closer to project_objective?
Is it semantically non-duplicative with the previous three phases?

answers.strategic_capabilities: Indirectly advances agent_loop, runtime_permission_enforcement, controlled_workspace_tools, and headless_autonomous_development by removing the documented diagnostics-wrapper blocker before M1.
answers.new_capability: Adds repository and CI/review enforcement that detects wrapper-only diagnostics API growth, long diagnostics names outside the legacy metadata, empty strategic capability mapping, and missing value-gate answers.
answers.why_existing_functionality_is_insufficie: PR #85 documented the desired guardrails, but Markdown cannot reject future code changes or CI runs by itself.
answers.product_gap_if_skipped: The phase loop could regress into more long diagnostics wrapper phases even after R1 documented that pattern as invalid.
answers.objective_alignment: It protects the roadmap so Brownie can return to the autonomous runtime, permission, tool, and headless execution capabilities named in the product objective.
answers.non_duplicative: Yes. R1.1 turns R1's planning documents into machine-checkable enforcement and deprecation metadata; it does not add another diagnostics wrapper.

reject_when: A PR adds no strategic capability and removes no documented blocker.
A PR only wraps existing diagnostics output in another endpoint, type, validator, or RuntimeClient method.
A PR cannot state a concrete user-visible or runtime capability gain.
A PR treats CI success as sufficient product value without answering the value gate.
A PR expands the diagnostics wrapper family outside the legacy allowlist.

rules: No new proposal.reviewQueueDiagnostics... RPC method may be added unless it already appears in the legacy allowlist.
No new ProposalReviewQueueDiagnostics... protocol type or RuntimeClient method may be added unless it is derived from an allowlisted legacy RPC.
Long diagnostics RPC or type names over the configured threshold must be legacy/deprecated metadata entries.
The current legacy family remains compatibility-only until a later consolidation phase removes or replaces it.
